title: fdid – User Tutorial
text:
Welcome
This Quarto book serves as a user tutorial for the fdid package in R, which implements the Factorial Difference-in-Differences (FDID) framework introduced by Xu, Zhao, and Ding (2026)[Paper]. This design is tailored for panel data settings where all units are exposed to a universal event but vary in a baseline factor \(G\) (currently supported as binary).
fdid provides two main functions:
And two S3 methods:

title: fdid – User Tutorial
text:
Organization
The user tutorial is structured into the following chapters:

Chapter 1  Get Started
This chapter covers installation instructions and introduces the dataset.
Chapter 2  FDID Main Program
This chapter explains how to prepare the dataset, perform factorial difference-in-differences estimation using several methods and plot the results.
Chapter 3  Visualization Options
This chapter explores more various plotting options.

title: 1  Get Started
text:
1.1 Installation
This chapter provides installation instructions and introduces the mortality dataset used in the tutorial.
To install fdid from CRAN, run the code chunk below:
install.packages("fdid")
The development version of the fdid package can be installed from Github:
# If not already installed
install.packages('devtools', repos = 'http://cran.us.r-project.org') 

# Directly install
devtools::install_github('xuyiqing/fdid')
If you downloaded the source folder and want to install locally, first build a .tar.gz file and then install it:
# It can be implemented by "Terminal" of R Studio:
# (Typing the command after ':' in each step at the 'Terminal' page 
# and replace "path/to/" with the actual path)
# 1. Set directory: cd path/to
# 2. Build package: R CMD build fdid
# 3. Install package: R CMD INSTALL fdid_0.1.0.tar.gz
fdid depends on the following packages, which should be installed automatically when fdid is being installed. You can also install them manually.
install_all <- function(packages) {
  installed_pkgs <- installed.packages()[, "Package"]
  for (pkg in packages) {
    if (!pkg %in% installed_pkgs) {
      install.packages(pkg)
    }
  }
}
packages <- c("estimatr", "dplyr", "tidyr", "rlang", "tidyselect", "RColorBrewer", 
              "foreach", "doParallel", "ebal", "grf", "car", 
              "sandwich")
install_all(packages)

title: 1  Get Started
text:
1.2 Dataset used in the tutorial
As in the empirical application of Xu, Zhao, and Ding (2026), We use the county-year panel from Cao, Xu, and Zhang (2022)[Paper] to illustrate the workflow.
Cao, Xu, and Zhang (2022) examines the role of social capital in disaster relief during China’s Great Famine and find that the rise in the mortality rate during the famine years is significantly less in counties with a higher clan density. In their research design, the event is China’s Great Famine (1958–1961), and the baseline factor \(G\) is social capital, measured by the density of genealogy books.
fdid ships this long-format panel dataset mortality, which works directly with fdid_prepare() we’ll explain in Chapter 2. To load it, run the code chunk below:

library(fdid)
data(fdid)
ls()
#> [1] "mortality"

The mortality dataset includes 16 variables. There are explanations of these variables.

Unit and period identifiers: provid identifies the province and countyid is the id of the county in this province; year indicates the period of the varibales.
Baseline factor in different measurement methods: pczupu is the number of genealogy books in a county; anyzupu is a binary variable which equals 1 if there’s any in genealogy book a county; zupu is a discrete variable to differentiate counties with some genealogies (zupu = 1) and counties with many genealogies (zupu = 2).
Outcome: mortality is a county’s mortality in a year.
Pre-event covariates:


avggrain: per capita grain production

nograin: ratio of non-farming land

urban: share of urban population

dis_bj: distance from Beijing (km)

dis_pc: distance from provincial capital (km)

rice: suitable for rice cultivation (indicator)

minority: share of ethnic minorities

edu: average years of education

lnpop: log county population




head(mortality)


  









Cao, Jiarui, Yiqing Xu, and Chuanchuan Zhang. 2022. “Clans and Calamity: How Social Capital Saved Lives During China’s Great Famine.” Journal of Development Economics 157: 102865.


Xu, Yiqing, Anqi Zhao, and Peng Ding. 2026. “Factorial Difference-in-Differences.” Journal of the American Statistical Association, no. just-accepted: 1–23.

title: 2  FDID Main Program
text:
2.1 Data preparation
In this chapter, we outline the complete workflow for an FDID analysis, which includes preparing the dataset, performing the estimation, and plotting the results.
Before calling fdid_prepare(), make sure
mortality$uniqueid <- paste(as.character(mortality$provid), as.character(mortality$countyid), sep = "-")
mortality$G <- ifelse(mortality$pczupu >= median(mortality$pczupu, na.rm = TRUE), 1, 0)
Next, define the key input parameters (event window, reference period, variable names):
tr_period <- 1958:1961
ref_period <- 1957
treat <- "G"
Y <- "mortality"
time <- "year"
unit <- "uniqueid"
covar <- c("avggrain", "nograin", "urban", "dis_bj", "dis_pc",
           "rice", "minority", "edu", "lnpop")
Finally, the user can use fdid_prepare() to reshape long-format panel data into a wide-format dataset and to construct the ingredients needed by fdid(). It also averages time-varying covariates and standardizes naming so downstream steps are consistent.
prepared_d <- fdid_prepare(
  data = mortality,
  Y_label = Y,
  X_labels = covar,
  G_label = treat,
  unit_label = unit,
  time_label = time
)
head(prepared_d)

title: 2  FDID Main Program
text:
each unit has a unique identifier across time;
the baseline factor \(G\) is coded as a 0/1 variable. To match the current implementation (binary \(G\)), we define


\(G=1\) for counties above the median social-capital measure;

\(G=0\) for counties below the median.















Currently, if the data are already in wide format, e.g. from the replication package of Xu, Zhao, and Ding (2026), the user needs to convert them to long format first.




2.1.1 Clustering if needed
Since the estimation program operates on the wide format and each row represents one unit, the robust standard errors we’re using are analogous to clustering at the unit level. However, if needed, the user can choose a different and higher level to cluster by passing the cluster variable (e.g. provid) to fdid_prepare(). Then fdid(..., vartype="robust") will report robust standard errors clustered at the higher level.

prepared_d_cl_prov <- fdid_prepare(
  data = mortality,
  Y_label = Y,
  X_labels = covar,
  G_label = treat,
  unit_label = unit,
  time_label = time,
  cluster_label = "provid"
)

title: 2  FDID Main Program
text:
2.2 Implementing FDID Analysis
fdid() performs factorial difference-in-differences (FDID) estimation using cross-sectional estimation methods based on first differences. It converts the \((Y_{i,t}, G_i, X_i)\) into a cross-sectional form \((\Delta Y_i, G_i, X_i)\) by comparing each period (or average of a time window) to a reference period. Compared with the TWFE model, our approach

forces each period-by-period estimation to use a balanced two-period comparison;
makes it easy to plug in selection-on-observables tools (stratification, balancing, matching, IPW, AIPW, outcome modeling, double machine learning, etc.).


2.2.1 Estimation Methods
Use method to choose an estimator:


"ols1": OLS with covariates but no interactions.

"ols2": OLS with covariates and interactions.

"did": OLS without covariates (i.e. 2$$2 DID).

"ebal": Entropy balancing.

"aipw": Augmented IPW via generalized random forests.


2.2.1.1 OLS without interactions
method = "ols1" corresponds to the \(OLS_+\) specification in Xu, Zhao, and Ding (2026): \[
\Delta Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_GG_i+\beta_X'\mathbf{X}_i+\varepsilon_i
\] where \(\Delta Y_i=Y_{i,*}-Y_{i,\text{ref}}\).
fdid() reports four summaries:

Pre-event average effect for all pre-event periods excluding the reference period: \(Y_{i,*}=\bar Y_{i,\text{pre}}\) where \(\bar Y_{i,\text{pre}}\) is the average of \(Y_{i,t}\) across all pre-event periods except the reference period.
Event-period average effect for all event periods: \(Y_{i,*}=\bar Y_{i,\text{event}}\) where \(\bar Y_{i,\text{event}}\) is the average of \(Y_{i,t}\) across all event periods.
Post-event average effect for all post-event periods: \(Y_{i,*}=\bar Y_{i,\text{post}}\) where \(\bar Y_{i,\text{post}}\) is the average of \(Y_{i,t}\) across all post-event periods.
Dynamic effects period-by-period relative to the reference: \(Y_{i,*}=Y_{i,t}\) for all \(t\).

How periods are defined: tr_period defines the event window. Every period before it is “pre”, every period after it is “post”. The reference period is separately set by ref_period and does not have to be the last pre-event period. If it’s set in event or post-event window, the corresponding window average effect will also exclude the reference period when calculating the average.

fdid_results_ols1 <- fdid(
  s = prepared_d,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  vartype = "robust",
)

summary() reports the point estimates, standard errors and 95% confidence intervals for the four summaries:

summary(fdid_results_ols1)

2.2.1.2 OLS with interactions
method = "ols2" corresponds to the \(OLS_*\) specification: \[
\Delta Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_GG_i+\beta_X'\mathbf{X}_i+\beta'_{GX}G_i\mathbf{X}_i+\varepsilon_i
\]
fdid() automatically centers covariates before estimation. With centered \(X_i\), the coefficient on \(G_i\) can be interpreted as consistent estimator for marginal estimand \(\tau_{\text{DID-X}}\) discussed in Xu, Zhao, and Ding (2026).

fdid_results_ols2 <- fdid(
  s = prepared_d,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "ols2",
  vartype = "robust",
)

2.2.1.3 Difference-in-Differences
method = 'did' is the no-covariate specification: \[
\Delta Y_i=\beta_0+\beta_G G_i+\varepsilon_i
\] Here, \(\beta_G\) is a cross-sectional analogue of the canonical \(2\times 2\) DID estimand.

fdid_results_did <- fdid(
  s = prepared_d,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "did",
  vartype = "robust",
)

2.2.1.4 Entropy Balancing
method = "ebal" reweights the \(G=0\) group so its covariate moments match the \(G=1\) group, and then estimates the effect on \(\Delta Y\). Because the weights are constructed to match the \(G=1\) group, this method currently targets ATT, so you must set ATT = TRUE.

fdid_results_ebal <- fdid(
  s = prepared_d,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "ebal",
  vartype = "robust",
  ATT = TRUE
)

2.2.1.5 Augmented IPW
method = "aipw" estimates unit-level effects using augmented inverse propensity score weighting (AIPW) with generalized random forests (GRF), and then aggregates them to produce ATE (or ATT if requested).

fdid_results_aipw <- fdid(
  s = prepared_d,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "aipw",
  vartype = "robust",
)

2.2.2 Variance Types
Use vartype to choose how standard errors are computed:

"robust": Heteroskedasticity-robust SE; becomes cluster-robust if you specified cluster_label in fdid_prepare().
"bootstrap": Bootstrap standard errors.
"jackknife": Leave-one-out jackknife standard errors.

Default: "robust".

2.2.2.1 Robust

fdid_results_ols1_cl <- fdid(
  s = prepared_d_cl_prov,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "ols1",
  vartype = "robust",
)

2.2.2.2 Bootstrap
You can use nsims to specify the number of simulations for bootstrap variance estimation (default:1000) and use cores to set the number of cores to speed up by parallel computing (default: 2).

fdid_results_ols1_boot <- fdid(
  s = prepared_d,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  vartype = "bootstrap",
  parallel = TRUE,
  cores = 6,
  nsims = 100
)


summary(fdid_results_ols1_boot)

2.2.3.3 missing_data

missing_data controls how missing outcomes are handled:


"listwise": Drop a unit if any required outcome is missing (more conservative).

"available": Drop a unit only if any group/covariates/cluster column is missing, keep units with partially missing outcomes whenever the comparison for a given period is feasible.

Default: "listwise".
In the example below, we create missing values to show how the two options differ.

d_missing <- d_package
# missing partial outcomes
d_missing$mortality[c(1:3, 101:102, 205:207)] <- NA
# missing covariates
d_missing$avggrain[1:100] <- NA
d_missing$edu[301:400] <- NA
prepared_data_missing <- fdid_prepare(
  data = d_missing,
  Y_label = Y,
  X_labels = covar,
  G_label = treat,
  unit_label = unit,
  time_label = time
)


fdid_results_ols1_missing_l <- fdid(
  s = prepared_data_missing,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "ols1",
  vartype = "robust",
  missing_data = "listwise"
)
fdid_results_ols1_missing_a <- fdid(
  s = prepared_data_missing,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "ols1",
  vartype = "robust",

)

title: 2  FDID Main Program
text:
2.6 Plotting the results
plot() provides visualizations for FDID estimation results:


type = "raw": Raw outcome means by group over time.

type = "dynamic": Dynamic effects relative to the reference period.

type = "overlap": Propensity-score overlap.

fdid_list() lets the user visually compare multiple fdid() results in one plot.

2.6.1 Raw means by group
Use type = "raw" to plot the outcome means and the 95% confidence intervals in each period for the \(G=0\) and \(G=1\) group. This plot is purely descriptive – it does not depend on the estimation method.

plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "raw")








2.6.2 Dynamic estimates
Use type = "dynamic" to plot period-by-period estimates relative to the reference period. These estimates come from separate cross-sectional estimations by year, not a single TWFE event-study regression.

plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "dynamic")








2.6.3 Diagnostic test for overlap
For method = "ipw" and method = "aipw", the user can assess the overlap assumption using type = "overlap", which plots the propensity score distributions by group.

plot(fdid_results_aipw, type = "overlap")








2.6.4 Comparison of methods
Use fdid_list() to collect multiple fdid() results and plot them together. The plot shows point estimates and 95% confidence intervals for each method.

plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_did, fdid_results_ols1, fdid_results_ols2, 
               fdid_results_ebal, fdid_results_ipw, fdid_results_aipw))













Cao, Jiarui, Yiqing Xu, and Chuanchuan Zhang. 2022. “Clans and Calamity: How Social Capital Saved Lives During China’s Great Famine.” Journal of Development Economics 157: 102865.


Xu, Yiqing, Anqi Zhao, and Peng Ding. 2026. “Factorial Difference-in-Differences.” Journal of the American Statistical Association, no. just-accepted: 1–23.

title: 2  FDID Main Program
text:
2.2 Logic of FDID analysis
fdid() performs factorial difference-in-differences (FDID) estimation using cross-sectional estimation methods based on first differences. It converts the \((Y_{i,t}, G_i, X_i)\) into a cross-sectional form \((\Delta Y_i, G_i, X_i)\) by comparing each period (or average of a time window) to a reference period.
fdid() reports both aggregate and dynamic estimates with different definition of \(Y_{i,*}\) in \(\Delta Y_i=Y_{i,*}-Y_{i,\text{ref}}\):

Aggregate estimates:

Pre-event average effect for all pre-event periods excluding the reference period: \(Y_{i,*}=\bar Y_{i,\text{pre}}\) where \(\bar Y_{i,\text{pre}}\) is the average of \(Y_{i,t}\) across all pre-event periods except the reference period.
Event-period average effect for all event periods: \(Y_{i,*}=\bar Y_{i,\text{event}}\) where \(\bar Y_{i,\text{event}}\) is the average of \(Y_{i,t}\) across all event periods.
Post-event average effect for all post-event periods: \(Y_{i,*}=\bar Y_{i,\text{post}}\) where \(\bar Y_{i,\text{post}}\) is the average of \(Y_{i,t}\) across all post-event periods.


Dynamic Estimates:

Dynamic effects period-by-period relative to the reference: \(Y_{i,*}=Y_{i,t}\) for all \(t\).









How periods are defined: tr_period defines the event window. Every period before it is “pre”, every period after it is “post”. The reference period is separately set by ref_period and does not have to be the last pre-event period. If it’s set in event or post-event window, the corresponding window average effect will also exclude the reference period when calculating the average.









Carryout effect: Because we cannot rule out carryover effects, all periods during or after the event should be considered treated or exposed. Therefore, setting a post-event period as the reference period is not recommended, even though we report separate estimates for the event and post-event periods.



Compared with the TWFE model, our approach

forces each period-by-period estimation to use a balanced two-period comparison;
makes it easy to plug in selection-on-observables tools (stratification, balancing, matching, IPW, AIPW, outcome modeling, double machine learning, etc.).







The reason why OLS is problematic here is the same as the issue with running a two-way fixed effects (TWFE) model using long-format data such as the violation of the no anticipation assumption.

2.5.2 entire_period

Use entire_period to restrict estimation to a subset of periods. This only changes which years enter the analysis – it does not change the reference period.

fdid_results_ols1_window <- fdid(
  s = prepared_d,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  entire_period = c(1958, 1959, 1960, 1961),
  method = "ols1",
  vartype = "robust",
)


summary(fdid_results_ols1_window)

2.5.3 missing_data

missing_data controls how missing outcomes are handled:


"listwise": Drop a unit if any required outcome is missing (more conservative).

"available": Drop a unit only if any group/covariates/cluster column is missing, keep units with partially missing outcomes whenever the comparison for a given period is feasible.

Default: "listwise".
In the example below, we create missing values to show how the two options differ.

d_missing <- d_package
# missing partial outcomes
d_missing$mortality[c(1:3, 101:102, 205:207)] <- NA
# missing covariates
d_missing$avggrain[1:100] <- NA
d_missing$edu[301:400] <- NA
prepared_data_missing <- fdid_prepare(
  data = d_missing,
  Y_label = Y,
  X_labels = covar,
  G_label = treat,
  unit_label = unit,
  time_label = time
)


fdid_results_ols1_missing_l <- fdid(
  s = prepared_data_missing,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "ols1",
  vartype = "robust",
  missing_data = "listwise"
)
fdid_results_ols1_missing_a <- fdid(
  s = prepared_data_missing,
  tr_period = tr_period,
  ref_period = ref_period,
  method = "ols1",
  vartype = "robust",
  missing_data = "available"
)


summary(fdid_results_ols1_missing_l)

title: 3  Visualization Options
text:
connected = TRUE connects points with lines (default: FALSE).




group_labels renames the two groups.




palette chooses an RColorBrewer palette (default: “Set2”).




alpha_shade controls the transparency of the shaded event window.



You can also set ylim, xlab, ylab, and main (currently, xlim is not supported).

title: 3  Visualization Options
text:
3.2 Options for dynamic estimates


connected = TRUE connects points with lines (default: FALSE).


plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "dynamic", connected = TRUE)









alpha_shade, ylim, xlab, ylab, and main customize the plot.


plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "dynamic", connected = TRUE,
     alpha_shade = 0.8, ylim = c(-10, 4),
     ylab = "Dynamic Moderating Effect", xlab = "Year", 
     main = "Dynamic Effect of High Social Capital"
     )









xlim zooms into a subset of years.


plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "dynamic",
     alpha_shade = 0.8, ylim = c(-10, 4), xlim = c(1954, 1961),
     ylab = "Dynamic Moderating Effect", xlab = "Year", 
     main = "Dynamic Effect of High Social Capital w/o Post-Event Periods"
     )

title: 3  Visualization Options
text:
3.3 Options for overlap test
You can adjust palette, ylim, xlab, and main:

plot(fdid_results_aipw, type = "overlap",
     palette = "Set1", ylim = c(-0.1, 0.1),
     xlab = 'Score', main = 'Diagnostic Test for Overlap Assumption'
     )

title: 3  Visualization Options
text:
3.4 Options for comparison of methods
By default, the comparison plot is horizontal. Set vertical = TRUE for a vertical layout.

plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_did, fdid_results_ols1, fdid_results_ols2, 
               fdid_results_ebal, fdid_results_aipw), vertical = TRUE
     )







Common options:

palette changes colors.
xlab and main change labels/titles.
ylim sets the range of the estimates axis.


plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_did, fdid_results_ols1, 
               fdid_results_ols2, fdid_results_ebal, fdid_results_aipw),
     palette = "Set1", xlab = 'Static Estimates',
     main = 'Comparing Differenct Methods', 
     ylim = c(-10,10)
     )








plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_did, fdid_results_ols1, fdid_results_ols2, 
               fdid_results_ebal, fdid_results_aipw), vertical = TRUE,
     ylim = c(-5,5)
     )







The labels reflect method, and add vartype when it is not the default "robust". Other settings (e.g., ATT = TRUE) are not currently shown in the labels.

plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_ols1, fdid_results_ols1_boot,
               fdid_results_ols1_jack, fdid_results_ols2, fdid_results_ols2_ATT,
               fdid_results_aipw),
     ylim = c(-5,1), vertical = TRUE
     )













Cao, Jiarui, Yiqing Xu, and Chuanchuan Zhang. 2022. “Clans and Calamity: How Social Capital Saved Lives During China’s Great Famine.” Journal of Development Economics 157: 102865.


Xu, Yiqing, Anqi Zhao, and Peng Ding. 2026. “Factorial Difference-in-Differences.” Journal of the American Statistical Association, no. just-accepted: 1–23.

title: 3  Visualization Options
text:
ci = FALSE removes the 95% confidence intervals.




connected = TRUE connects points with lines (default: FALSE).




group_labels renames the two groups.




palette chooses an RColorBrewer palette (default: “Set2”).




shade_periods controls the position of shaded region. Default is x$tr_period, i.e. the event window.

By setting shade_periods = c(1954, 1957), the pre-event periods can be set shaded.
By setting shade_periods = NULL, the user can also remove the shaded region.







alpha_shade controls the transparency of the shaded region.



The user can also set ylim, xlab, ylab, and main (currently, xlim is not supported).

title: 3  Visualization Options
text:
3.2 Dynamic estimates


ci = FALSE removes the 95% confidence intervals.


plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "dynamic", ci = FALSE)









connected = TRUE connects points with lines (default: FALSE).


plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "dynamic", connected = TRUE)









shade_periods, alpha_shade, ylim, xlab, ylab, and main customize the plot as above.


plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "dynamic", connected = TRUE,
     shade_periods = c(1954, 1957),
     alpha_shade = 0.8, ylim = c(-10, 4),
     ylab = "Dynamic Moderating Effect", xlab = "Year", 
     main = "Dynamic Effect of High Social Capital"
     )









xlim zooms into a subset of years.


plot(fdid_results_ols1, type = "dynamic",
     alpha_shade = 0.8, ylim = c(-10, 4), xlim = c(1954, 1961),
     ylab = "Dynamic Moderating Effect", xlab = "Year", 
     main = "Dynamic Effect of High Social Capital w/o Post-Event Periods"
     )

3.3 Overlap test
The user can adjust palette, ylim, xlab, and main:

plot(fdid_results_aipw, type = "overlap",
     palette = "Set1", ylim = c(-0.1, 0.1),
     xlab = 'Score', main = 'Diagnostic Test for Overlap Assumption'
     )

title: 3  Visualization Options
text:
3.4 Comparison of methods
By default, the comparison plot shows vertical estimates and CIs (i.e. a horizontal layout). Set vertical = FALSE for horizontal estimates and CIs.

plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_did, fdid_results_ols1, fdid_results_ols2, 
               fdid_results_ebal, fdid_results_aipw), vertical = FALSE
     )







Common options:

palette changes colors.
xlab and main change labels/titles.
ylim sets the range of the estimates axis.


plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_did, fdid_results_ols1, fdid_results_ols2, 
               fdid_results_ebal, fdid_results_aipw),
     ylim = c(-5,5)
     )








plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_did, fdid_results_ols1, 
               fdid_results_ols2, fdid_results_ebal, fdid_results_aipw),
     palette = "Set1", xlab = 'Static Estimates',
     main = 'Comparing Differenct Methods', vertical = FALSE,
     ylim = c(-10,10)
     )







The labels reflect method, and add vartype when it is not the default "robust". Other settings (e.g., target.pop) are not currently shown in the labels.

plot(fdid_list(fdid_results_ols1, fdid_results_ols1_boot,
               fdid_results_ols1_jack, fdid_results_ols2, fdid_results_ols2_target_1,
               fdid_results_aipw),
     ylim = c(-5,1), vertical = TRUE
     )
